_note: Plain-English formation explainers for fans who see the numbers but want the real idea behind them.

formations.plainEnglish: Four defenders, three midfielders, three attackers.
formations.whyCoachesUseIt: It gives natural width, a clear midfield triangle, and an easy platform for pressing.
formations.whatFansWillNotice: The wingers stay high, the central midfielders rotate, and the fullbacks often help create overloads.
tradeoffs: can leave the lone striker isolated
demands a lot from the wide forwards
formations.bestFor: Teams that want to attack without losing structure.

formations.plainEnglish: Four defenders, two deeper midfielders, three creators behind one striker.
formations.vibe: Stable with room for a star playmaker.
formations.whyCoachesUseIt: The double pivot adds security while the attacking midfielder gets freedom between the lines.
formations.whatFansWillNotice: The team often looks compact without the ball and much more layered once it settles in possession.
strengths: clear balance between risk and safety
tradeoffs: can get flat if the attacking line does not move enough
relies heavily on the lone striker to occupy defenders
formations.bestFor: Teams built around one main creator or a careful tournament approach.

formations.plainEnglish: Four defenders, four midfielders, two forwards.
formations.vibe: Classic, disciplined, and easy to defend in.
formations.whyCoachesUseIt: It keeps two banks of four compact and gives the team two reference points up front.
formations.whatFansWillNotice: The shape is simple to read, especially when a side is defending deep and trying to counter quickly.
strengths: two strikers can occupy both center-backs
tradeoffs: creative burden can fall too heavily on wide players
formations.bestFor: Underdogs, organized mid-block teams, and direct transition sides.

formations.plainEnglish: Three center-backs, five across midfield, and two forwards.
formations.vibe: Compact through the middle with aggressive wing-backs.
formations.whyCoachesUseIt: It protects the center while still allowing width from the wing-backs and partnerships up front.
formations.whatFansWillNotice: The wing-backs become the most exhausting job on the field because they have to create and recover constantly.
strengths: natural rest defense with three center-backs
tradeoffs: width disappears if the flank players tire
formations.bestFor: Teams that trust their central defenders and want to attack with two forwards.

formations.plainEnglish: Four defenders, one sitter, four across midfield, one striker.
formations.vibe: Control-first and difficult to play through.
formations.whyCoachesUseIt: The lone holding midfielder protects the center while the line of four ahead can press or drop depending on game state.
formations.whatFansWillNotice: It often looks conservative at first, then surprisingly aggressive when the midfield line jumps together.
strengths: easy to turn into a block
tradeoffs: creative players need runners around them
formations.bestFor: Tournament teams that care about structure first and flair second.
